meta.title: Traffic Management Plan — CDM 2015 Reg 27 / Schedule 3
meta.subtitle: Meadowbank Road site — vehicle/pedestrian segregation, delivery & reversing controls
meta.author: Construction Logistics / Temporary Works (Traffic) Coordinator
meta.classification: Traffic management plan — competent-person review required
meta.deid_notice: This is a site/route-level plan and carries no named individual. Any driver, operative, or banksman in the source inputs was scrubbed to a role/location label before drafting; the re-identification key is held separately and is not part of this document.

sections.text: Traffic management plan for the named Meadowbank Road site: a north gate for HGV deliveries and a separate south gate for operatives. The principal conflict point is the north loading bay, where reversing HGVs cross the operative route to the welfare cabins. A footway runs adjacent to the north hoarding (public interface). Routes are planned to be suitable, sufficient and separated (CDM 2015 Schedule 3).

metrics.label: Conflict points
metrics.caption: north loading bay, welfare crossing, north footway

rows: Crosses operative welfare route at the north bay — SEGREGATED below
Shared route with operatives — physically separated below
South gate → segregated walkway → work areas / welfare
Kept off the vehicle area by design
Protected by hoarding + covered way (not a banksman)
sections.caption: Named routes — the plan refuses a generic 'site roads' layout.

sections.text: Segregation-by-design LEADS, NOT a banksman. The site's proposed 'operatives wear hi-vis and a banksman waves the HGVs in' pedestrian control is rejected as a PPE/admin-only treatment (controls.ppe_admin_only = True, no higher-order control, no justification) and pushed up the hierarchy: a segregated pedestrian walkway behind barriers removes operatives from the vehicle area entirely; the banksman is retained only as the LAST-resort residual control for the final manoeuvre.

rows.control: Remove pedestrians from the vehicle area — barriered, segregated walkway from the south gate to the work areas and welfare; gated crossing where it must cross a vehicle route
Physical segregation — pedestrian barriers and edge protection along the shared forklift route; separate vehicle / people site access
Signage, speed limit and lighting at the welfare crossing (warning of approach, Schedule 3)
Banksman / traffic marshal — the LAST resort, only for the final reversing manoeuvre into the bay once one-way and segregation are designed in

sections.text: Reversing is ELIMINATED by design, not managed by a banksman. The HGV route is made one-way (north gate in, separate exit) with a turning circle / drive-through loading bay so vehicles do not reverse into the work area. Where a residual reversing manoeuvre remains, a trained banksman is the last-resort control — never the headline.

items: One-way HGV circulation: enter north gate, drive through the loading bay, exit — no reversing into the work area.
Turning circle provided so vehicles turn rather than reverse across the operative route.
Banksman / traffic marshal retained only for the final set-down manoeuvre, after the one-way and segregation are in place.
Deliveries booked into timed windows to remove queueing and ad-hoc reversing on the highway.
Dedicated loading / unloading bay and a holding area off the public road for early arrivals.
Routing to and from the bay fixed and signed; no delivery reverses across a pedestrian route.

rows: 5 mph speed limit, signed at the gate
Vehicle-emerging warning + wheel-wash to keep the highway clean
sections.caption: Routes signed and lit at conflict points (Schedule 3 warning of approach).

sections.text: The public is NEVER controlled by a banksman alone. The footway adjacent to the north hoarding is protected by a continuous hoarding and a covered way; a vehicle-emerging warning and (where the highway authority requires it) the relevant traffic-signing duty manage the gate / footway interface.

items: Continuous hoarding + covered way protect the public footway from site traffic and falling materials.
Vehicle-emerging signage and a banksman ASSIST the gate egress — but the public segregation is physical, not a banksman.
Highway-signing duty applied where the live-highway interface requires it.

rows.action: Daily route inspection and near-miss capture on the vehicle/pedestrian interfaces; brief every operative and delivery driver on the TMP
Audit the one-way system and segregation barriers weekly; correct any breach of the segregated walkway
Re-plan and re-brief the TMP on any change of construction phase, site access, traffic type, or delivery profile
Review the TMP against the live site at each phase transition

sections.text: Decision-support only. This traffic management plan must be reviewed by a competent person before it is implemented on site; it does not constitute competent-person approval.

items: CDM 2015 Regulation 27 — organisation of the site so pedestrians and vehicles can move safely
CDM 2015 Schedule 3 — traffic routes: suitable, sufficient and separated, with warning of approach
HSE HSG144 — The safe use of vehicles on construction sites
ISO 45001 clause 6.1.2 / 8.1.2 — hazard ID and the hierarchy of controls
Segregation-by-design hierarchy (KB-SNIP-TRAFFIC-SEGREGATION): eliminate / one-way & turning / physical segregation / signage / banksman LAST
